AppUsage.title: AppUsage
AppUsage.description: Tracks the user's daily usage of the AI reply generation feature to manage freemium access.

id.description: Unique identifier for the AppUsage entity. This is a singleton entity, so its ID will typically be a fixed string like 'appUsage'.

count.description: The number of AI replies generated by the user on the current day, reset daily.

lastResetDate.description: The calendar date (YYYY-MM-DD) when the usage count was last reset or updated. Used to determine if a new day has started for count reset.

AppState.title: AppState
AppState.description: Stores application-wide settings and flags, such as the user's onboarding status.

id.description: Unique identifier for the AppState entity. This is a singleton entity, so its ID will typically be a fixed string like 'appState'.

onboarded.description: A flag indicating whether the user has completed the initial onboarding carousel. 'true' means onboarding has been shown, 'false' means it should be shown.

ReplyHistoryEntry.title: ReplyHistoryEntry
ReplyHistoryEntry.description: Represents a single session of AI reply generation, storing the user's inputs, selected tones, and the four generated replies.

id.description: Unique identifier for the ReplyHistoryEntry entity.

platform.description: The communication platform selected by the user for this generation (e.g., 'Instagram DM', 'WhatsApp', 'Tinder/Bumble').

inputMessage.description: The original message text pasted by the user. This is optional if a situation description is provided instead.

inputSituation.description: A descriptive context of the situation provided by the user. This is optional if an original message is pasted.

yourName.description: Optional personal context about the user (e.g., 'Your name / what they know about you') provided for AI generation.

selectedToneIds.description: An array of identifiers for the communication tones selected by the user for this generation (e.g., 'funny_witty', 'flirty_subtle').

generatedReplies.description: An array containing the four AI-generated replies, each with its text, tone label, and a brief explanation of why it works.

timestamp.description: The exact date and time (ISO 8601 format) when this reply generation session was recorded, used for ordering history.

WaitlistEntry.title: WaitlistEntry
WaitlistEntry.description: Stores information about a user who has opted into the waitlist for unlimited access to the app's features.

id.description: Unique identifier for the WaitlistEntry entity.

email.description: The email address provided by the user to join the waitlist.

timestamp.description: The exact date and time (ISO 8601 format) when the user's email was added to the waitlist.

definition.description: Stores the user's daily AI reply generation count and last reset date. This is a singleton document per user. Authorization is path-based; only the authenticated user can access their own document.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user.

definition.description: Stores application-wide settings and flags specific to a user, such as onboarding status. This is a singleton document per user. Authorization is path-based; only the authenticated user can access their own document.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user.

definition.description: Stores individual AI reply generation sessions for a user, including inputs, selected tones, and generated replies. Authorization is path-based; only the authenticated user can access their own reply history entries. Supports secure `list` operations for user's history.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user who owns this reply history entry.

params.name: replyHistoryEntryId
params.description: The unique identifier for a specific reply generation session.

firestore.reasoning: The primary request is to design a Firestore structure based on the provided `Application Data Entities`. However, there's a strong contradiction in the 'App requirements' stating 'All data in localStorage (no backend needed)' and specifically moving `AppUsage`, `AppState`, `ReplyHistoryEntry`, and `WaitlistEntry` to `localStorage` for the PWA implementation.My interpretation resolves this by assuming that the `backend.entities` section defines the canonical data model for a hypothetical backend, and the Firestore structure is requested *as if* these entities (barring specific `localStorage` mandates) were to be stored in a backend. If the application were to scale beyond client-side storage, this structure would be applicable.Based on this interpretation, the `WaitlistEntry` entity is explicitly marked for `localStorage` in the app requirements and is therefore excluded from the Firestore structure. The other entities (`AppUsage`, `AppState`, `ReplyHistoryEntry`) are designed for Firestore storage, assuming a future or alternative backend context.Design Justification & Core Principles Adherence:1.  Authorization Independence (CRITICAL):The structure achieves authorization independence by modeling all user-specific data under a dedicated user path: `/users/{userId}/...`. For example, `AppUsage` is stored at `/users/{userId}/appUsage`, `AppState` at `/users/{userId}/appState`, and `ReplyHistoryEntry` at `/users/{userId}/replyHistory/{replyHistoryEntryId}`. This design ensures that the `userId` required for authorization is inherently part of the document's path. Consequently, Firestore security rules can directly compare `request.auth.uid` with the `{userId}` wildcard in the path (e.g., `allow read, write: if request.auth.uid == userId;`). This eliminates the need for expensive and complex `get()` calls to other documents or parent collections to determine ownership or permissions, making operations atomic, faster, and rules easier to write, understand, and debug.2.  Clarity of Intent (Debuggability):The path structure clearly indicates ownership and scope. For instance, `/users/{userId}/replyHistory/{replyHistoryEntryId}` explicitly states that `replyHistoryEntryId` belongs to `userId`. This makes authorization intent explicit: access to any document under `/users/{userId}/` is strictly limited to the authenticated user whose UID matches `userId`.3.  DBAC (No Custom Claims):Authorization relies solely on `request.auth.uid` and path-based ownership. No custom claims are needed as all role-like information (i.e., ownership) is derived from the database structure itself.4.  QAPs (Rules are not Filters):The design inherently supports secure `list` operations. When a user queries their `replyHistory` (`/users/{userId}/replyHistory`), the security rules ensure that `request.auth.uid` matches the `userId` in the path. This means that a client's `list` query for `/users/{someOtherUserId}/replyHistory` will be denied by the rules, enforcing that users can only list *their own* data. The client-side query must specify `request.auth.uid` for `userId`, and the rules will validate it, preventing data leakage or accidental over-fetching of unauthorized data. This aligns with QAPs by making the query inherently secure and not relying on security rules to filter results *after* a potentially broader query.5.  Invariants:Ownership is maintained structurally through the `users/{userId}` path segment. Timestamps (e.g., `timestamp` in `ReplyHistoryEntry`) are part of the document schema, and their integrity can be enforced with rules (e.g., `request.resource.data.timestamp == request.time`).This structure prioritizes security and simplifies rule management by leveraging Firestore's hierarchical nature for explicit, performant, and debuggable access control.
